tasks.title: Merge PR #1 — release workflow → OIDC trusted publishing
tasks.sub: No more long-lived npm secrets in CI.

learn.before: Trusted publishing swaps the stored npm token for an OIDC handshake: CI proves who it is to npm, per release. Nothing long-lived is left to leak.

steps.text: Merge the PR
Watch the release workflow go green
steps.concept_text: Instead of a secret sitting in CI, npm and GitHub do a per-run identity handshake. A leaked log can never leak a token that does not exist.

capture.question: After this merge, what lives in CI secrets?
choices: Nothing npm-related — the OIDC handshake is the credential
capture.why: The handshake is the credential — no stored token exists to steal.

tasks.title: Merge PR #2 — schema domain → schema.workspec.io/v1alpha1
tasks.sub: The schema gets its permanent public home.

learn.before: The schema moves to a domain you own. Hosting can change underneath it forever after; the URL in every $schema line does not.

steps.text: Merge the PR
Confirm CI publishes under /v1alpha1

capture.question: Why give the schema its own subdomain?
choices: GitHub Pages requires a custom domain
The URL must outlive wherever the files happen to be hosted
capture.why: Hosting moves; a URL you own doesn’t.

learn.before: A CNAME says “this name is an alias for that name.” You’re telling DNS that schema.workspec.io answers wherever GitHub Pages answers. Once GitHub sees the record it provisions a TLS certificate on its own.

steps.text: Add the record at your registrar
steps.concept_label: why alias a name, not an IP
steps.concept_text: GitHub’s IPs change without notice. The alias follows them automatically; a hardcoded A record would break silently.

steps.text: Wait out propagation — minutes to an hour
steps.concept_text: Nothing is pushed anywhere — resolvers cache answers, and propagation is just those caches expiring. The record’s TTL decides how long stale answers linger.

steps.text: Confirm it resolves

capture.question: Why a CNAME here — not an A record?
choices: GitHub’s IPs can change; the alias follows them, a hardcoded IP wouldn’t
capture.why: HTTPS comes from the certificate, not the record type — the alias is about GitHub’s changing IPs.

tasks.title: Point GitHub Pages at the custom domain
tasks.sub: One gh api call — HTTPS enforced.

learn.before: Pages needs to know the domain claims it; then it requests a certificate and can force HTTPS. One API call sets all of it.

steps.text: Set the cname + enforce HTTPS
steps.cmd: gh api -X PUT repos/fieldstatenz/decision-studio/pages -f cname=schema.workspec.io -F https_enforced=true

steps.text: Wait for the cert check to go green
steps.concept_text: GitHub asks Let’s Encrypt for a certificate once the CNAME is visible, then renews it forever. You never touch a key.

capture.question: What does https_enforced=true actually do?
choices: Redirects http → https once the cert exists
capture.why: The record proves ownership; enforcement is just a redirect GitHub flips on once the cert exists.

tasks.title: Register npm trusted publishers — 4 packages
tasks.sub: Four packages, same three fields each.

learn.before: Each package tells npm which repo and which workflow may publish it. That mapping is the whole trust model — no tokens anywhere.

steps.text: Add the publisher on each of the 4 packages
Double-check the workflow filename matches release.yml

capture.question: What exactly does npm verify at publish time?
choices: That the build came from the registered repo + workflow
A GPG signature on the tarball
capture.why: The registered repo + workflow pair is the identity; provenance falls out of it.

tasks.title: Verify $schema URL + IntelliSense from a clean editor

learn.before: A clean editor profile is the only honest test: no cached schema, no logged-in state — exactly what a stranger gets.

steps.text: Open a scratch workspec in a fresh profile
Confirm completions come from schema.workspec.io

capture.question: Why test from a clean editor profile?
choices: Your cache can serve a stale schema and hide a broken URL
IntelliSense only works in fresh profiles
capture.why: Caches lie on your machine only — a stranger’s editor has no cache to save it.

tasks.title: Decide: rename API_VERSION to match the new domain?
tasks.sub: Breaking to every artifact — decide before adoption.

learn.before: The string workspec.fieldstate.io/v1alpha1 is stamped into every artifact. Today a rename costs one regeneration; after the first outside adopter it costs them, forever.

decision_options.title: Rename now → schema.workspec.io/v1alpha1
decision_options.body: Breaking today, clean forever. Every internal artifact regenerates once.

decision_options.title: Keep workspec.fieldstate.io/v1alpha1
decision_options.body: No churn now; the domain mismatch becomes permanent lore.

capture.question: Why does this decision expire at the first outside adopter?
choices: npm locks the name at first publish
After adoption the string is a public contract — changing it breaks them
capture.why: Before adoption a rename is free; after, it’s someone else’s breakage.

tasks.title: Cut the first release
tasks.sub: npm login once, then the pipeline publishes.

learn.before: First publish bootstraps the packages; every later release is CI-only via the trusted publisher.

steps.text: Login once
Tag and let the pipeline publish

capture.question: Why is npm login needed only this once?
choices: First publish creates the package; after that the trusted publisher takes over
It isn’t — every release needs it
capture.why: Creation needs a human; publishing after that is the workflow’s job.

tasks.title: Decide: keep the no-self-merge review guard?
tasks.sub: Two-minute call; friction on every future merge.

learn.before: You merge everything yourself. The guard forces the PR ritual even when the reviewer is you.

decision_options.title: Keep the guard
decision_options.body: Every merge takes the PR path. Slower, safer at 2am.

decision_options.title: Relax for green-CI PRs
decision_options.body: Solo-merge when checks pass; discipline moves to the pipeline.

capture.question: What does the guard actually protect against?
capture.why: It’s a seatbelt against your own hurry, not against other people.

tasks.title: Provide a live Anthropic API key + pick a staging target
tasks.sub: Last human input before live validation.

learn.before: Issue #27 needs real model calls. A scoped key in the staging vault plus a named target is everything the pipeline is waiting on.

steps.text: Create a scoped key in the console
Drop it in the staging vault
Name the target
steps.concept_text: #27 exercises live calls with real budgets. Staging keeps a bad prompt from becoming a bad bill.

capture.question: Why does #27 need a live key when mocks pass?
choices: #27 validates real model behavior — mocks can’t, by definition
The SDK requires one in staging
capture.why: Mocks validate plumbing; the issue exists to validate behavior.

tasks.title: Say “go” — dev lead pre-builds the lm2 harness now
tasks.sub: One word; it builds while you sleep.

learn.before: The offer on the table: build the harness before your key lands, so lm1 unblocks everything the moment it arrives.

decision_options.title: Go — build it now
decision_options.body: Harness lands tonight; lm1 becomes the last domino.

decision_options.title: Wait for the key first
decision_options.body: Serial and safe; nothing speculative gets built.

capture.question: What does “go” actually buy?
choices: The harness builds in parallel, so your key unblocks everything instantly
capture.why: It converts a serial wait into a parallel one — the classic cheap win.

tasks.title: This design brief → Claude Design session

learn.before: The externalized mental model, being externalized. This session is the task.

steps.text: Review the direction options
Settle on one — lanes of one, sidebar-width
Take delivery of the hi-fi prototype

capture.question: What makes Slipway not a todo app?
choices: Execution is the method; retained capability is the point
capture.why: The checkmark isn’t the product — what you can still do a month later is.

tasks.title: Dogfood: run both launches through Slipway
tasks.sub: Run the Loom + DS launches through Slipway itself.

question.question: You drop a TTL from 3600 to 300 right before a cutover. Why?
choices: Lower TTL makes DNS resolve faster
Caches expire sooner, so the switch lands in minutes, not hours
It forces resolvers to re-register the record
question.why: TTL is just cache lifetime — shorter cache, faster convergence.
